text: The surgeon divides the falciform ligament and the left triangular ligament, enabling retraction of the left lateral section and part of the left lobe of the liver towards the right side of the patient.
The surgeon divides the lesser omentum, specifically the pars flaccidae, revealing the chordate process and the tumor beneath.

A Nathanson liver retractor is positioned beneath the left lateral section of the liver, enhancing exposure. The surgeon dissects the peritoneal reflections originating from the diaphragm and joining the lesser omentum.

The surgeon completes the opening of the pars flaccida and divides the lesser omentum up to the diaphragm level. A golf ball-sized tumor is visible inferiorly in the caudate process, while the superior caudate process appears relatively normal.
The surgeon dissects and divides the ligamentum venosum near its insertion into the leptopatic vein, revealing the anatomical structures in the surgical field.

The surgeon elevates and retracts the tissue inferiorly to resect the entire fat pad, including the ligament. Additional adhesions near the portal origin of the ligamentum venosum are dissected, and the ligament is exposed and divided using an energy device.
The surgeon sets aside the resected tissue, preparing it to be placed into a specimen bag for retrieval.

Fatty adhesions between the lesser omental fat and the chordate lobe are visible, with the tumor encapsulated within these adhesions. The adhesions are left intact to avoid breaching the tumor capsule, ensuring a clear surgical margin.
The hepatic artery is visible inferiorly, pulsating prominently in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully navigates around a rounded tumor, avoiding excessive pressure due to the soft nature of hepatocellular carcinomas. The inferior vena cava and the first chordate tributary are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the bridge of the caudate process crossing behind the porta hepatis and in front of the inferior vena cava.
The surgeon approaches the portal inflow to the chordate process, dividing superficial tissue to lengthen the area. A small branch is torn and grasped, then controlled using bipolar diathermy.
The surgeon uses an energy device to cauterize small vessels in the limited space, avoiding the use of clips to prevent congestion, in preparation for using a stapler later in the operation.
The surgeon carefully dissects and lengthens the vessels to safely transect them using an energy device, while avoiding damage to the inflow to the left lobe of the liver.

The surgeon dissects extra peritoneal attachments to the superior portion of the chordate process, gradually freeing the chordate process and tumor on three of four sides, leaving a bridge of chordate parenchyma to be stapled through.

The surgeon successfully lengthens the tissue between the porta hepatis and the chordate process, exposing the bridge of chordate parenchyma behind the porta hepatis. The bridge is visibly divided on the patient's left side.

The surgical field shows small tributaries of the cava plugging into the bridge of chordate parenchyma. The surgeon clips and controls a vessel causing annoying ooze, using suction to maintain visibility in the field.

A gauze square is placed to control the ooze as the surgeon approaches the inferior vena cava on its supralateral aspect. The retrocaval ligament is being developed, revealing a vessel located very laterally at the start of the ligament.

The surgeon identifies a vessel that is challenging to access in open surgery due to the chordate process obstructing the view, but it is more accessible during laparoscopic surgery. The chordate process is elevated, revealing its complex shape, which poses difficulty in manipulation.

A retracting instrument is used to manipulate the chordate lobe, which is densely adhered to the inferior vena cava through ligamentous wrappings, short hepatic veins, and peritoneal reflections. The surgeon gradually addresses these attachments to elevate the superior aspect of the chordate lobe anteriorly, preparing a landing zone for the stapler.
The surgeon identifies a short hepatic vein in front of the inferior vena cava, clips it, and then divides it using an energy device to facilitate safe passage of the stapler across the cordate lobe.
The surgeon carefully dissects the areolar tissue in front of the inferior vena cava, ensuring not to avulse any tributaries, while releasing the posterior superior corner.

The surgeon manipulates the caudate process, thinning down the pre-caval bridge behind the porta hepatis while controlling small oozing vessels. Retraction is used to achieve adequate length for stapler passage, avoiding pinching the tumor.

The surgeon passes anteriorly to the left portal structures, thinning down the tissue to prevent the stapler from pulling in the tumor or unwanted structures, while managing minor oozing from the tissue.
The surgical team actively uses a suction device to manage bleeding while the surgeon prepares the stapler, thinning down tissue and controlling small oozing vessels in the operative field.

The surgeon lifts the caudate process forward, which stops the venous bleeding. A stapler is then passed at the same angle as the suction device, ensuring it does not pinch the inferior vena cava or include the tumor.

The surgeon uses the stapler as a crushing device to squeeze fluid out and thin the parenchyma, allowing the stapler to advance slightly further. The entire 60-millimeter vascular load of the stapler is utilized.

The surgeon compresses the tissue with the stapler for thirty to sixty seconds before firing it. A second stapler is introduced to complete the transection, leaving a small bridge of tissue in front of the inferior vena cava. Care is taken to ensure the tumor remains outside the staple line.

The surgeon positions the stapler near the diaphragm and pericardium, ensuring it does not advance further. The specimen is temporarily set aside while the surgical field is checked for any significant bleeding, which appears minimal.

The surgeon places the fat from around the chest ligament with the specimen and introduces a bag for specimen removal. The specimen is removed through an incisional hernia site, avoiding pelvic adhesions. After cleaning, fibrillar is applied to control residual oozing.

The surgeon uses a suction device to explore the surgical area, identifying a vessel that has opened up, likely an arterial branch. Despite limited exposure, metallic clips are applied to control the bleeding, ensuring the vessel is secured without the use of a stapler.

The surgeon places a stapler around the tissue without needing to encircle it completely, unlike locking clips. After ensuring all bleeding has stopped, additional fibrillar is applied to address mesenteric ooze from adhesiolysis. The abdomen is closed without a drain.
